title: Gratuitous ARP
description: An ARP reply sent without a prior request — used to announce or update IP-to-MAC mappings on the network.
fullExplanation: A Gratuitous ARP (GARP) is an ARP reply where the sender IP and target IP are both set to the sender's own IP address. It's sent without any prior ARP request. Devices use GARPs to: announce their presence when they come online, update ARP caches after a MAC address change (e.g. NIC replacement), and facilitate failover in high-availability setups (HSRP, VRRP, CARP). Because ARP has no authentication, GARPs can also be abused for ARP poisoning attacks.

useCases.description: Device sends GARP on startup. If another device replies, there's an IP conflict.
After NIC replacement, GARP updates all neighbors' ARP caches with new MAC.
When standby router becomes active, it sends GARP to redirect traffic to its MAC.
After VM moves to new host, GARP updates switches and neighbors with new MAC.
Attacker sends GARP claiming to be the gateway — poisons ARP caches for MITM.

keyFacts: GARP: sender IP = target IP = own IP address
Sent to broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) — all devices update their ARP cache
No authentication — any device can send a GARP claiming any IP
HSRP uses GARPs when active router changes
Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) can validate GARPs against DHCP snooping table
Windows sends 3 GARPs on startup; Linux sends 1 by default
